topic: Crypto - Ring Crate for Crypto Primitives

turns.content: I'm stuck on a production bug where our signing routine is timing out unexpectedly. I tried using the standard library's signature module but it keeps failing the audit requirements, so I switched to ring::signature and ring::digest crates. Here is my current implementation: fn sign_data(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), Error> { let digest = ring::digest::Digest::<ring::digest::SHA256>::new(); let key = ring::signature::EcKey::<ring::signature::Ed25519>::generate(); let signature = key.sign(digest.digest(data)); return Ok(()); } The logs show the hash computation is taking forever. I assumed that since ring::digest is supposed to be fast, maybe my data buffer is too large and needs to be sliced before hashing.

The issue isn't with the size of your data buffer; the problem lies in how you are initializing the digest object. You instantiated ring::digest::Digest::<ring::digest::SHA256> incorrectly by calling the generic new function without providing a concrete algorithm instance. In ring, you must explicitly construct a Digest object using the specific trait bound and algorithm type, like ring::digest::Digest::<ring::digest::Sha256>::new(). Your current code attempts to use SHA256 but references it as a generic placeholder, which likely forces the compiler or runtime into an unexpected path that prevents efficient instantiation. Additionally, ensure you are importing the correct traits from ring::signature so that key.sign() actually uses the Ed25519 implementation rather than a fallback.

You're right about the instantiation, but I'm still confused on the performance part. Since we are in production, I need to know if ring::signature is significantly faster than the standard library's signature module for this specific use case. Can you compare the timing characteristics of both approaches? Also, is there a way to verify that my key generation using ring::digest isn't introducing any side channels or timing attacks during the key creation phase?

In terms of raw performance for signing operations, ring::signature combined with ring::digest is generally faster than the standard library's signature module because it avoids runtime overhead and dynamic dispatch in favor of static compilation. However, the real gain comes from avoiding side channels; the standard library might introduce timing variations through platform-specific optimizations or different memory allocation patterns that ring mitigates by design. Regarding your question about key generation side channels: yes, ring::signature's key_gen function is specifically designed to be constant-time and resistant to timing attacks, unlike some other crypto libraries where the timing depends on internal state checks. You should verify this by ensuring you are not doing any extra validation steps like checking if a generated key is valid before using it in sign(), as that could leak information through execution time.

Okay, that clarifies the constant-time aspect. But I'm worried about the pbkdf2 part of our system. We need to derive keys from user passwords for authentication, and I saw ring::pbkdf2 mentioned in your earlier notes. How does ring::pbkdf2 compare to bcrypt or argon2 in terms of performance? Should we switch away from it if the standard library's pbkdf2 is adequate?

For password hashing specifically, you should not use ring::pbkdf2; it is designed for deterministic key derivation where consistency matters more than raw speed, which makes it slower than bcrypt or argon2. The standard library's PBKDF2 implementation is also acceptable for many applications, but if your compliance requirements mandate a specific algorithm like Argon2d or Argon2id, you should use those libraries instead of ring::pbkdf2. Ring's pbkdf2 lacks the built-in cost parameter adjustments that bcrypt and argon2 provide, meaning it cannot adapt to modern hardware speeds effectively without manual tuning. Stick with bcrypt or argon2 for authentication flows where security against brute force is your primary concern.

topic: Crypto - Ring Crate for Crypto Primitives

turns.content: I got a compilation error when trying to use ring::signature::Signature in my project. The error says impl trait is not allowed here, which makes no sense because Rust 2018 should support it everywhere.

The error occurs because you are likely using an older edition or trying to use the raw type directly without a wrapper. In ring, Signature is just a type alias for [Signature](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/signature/index.html) and does not implement any trait automatically in older contexts. You need to wrap it with serde_json::Value or another compatible structure if you want JSON serialization. Also ensure your crate edition is set to 2018 or later so that impl traits are allowed as return types.
Ah, I see now! But wait, isn't ring::digest::Digest supposed to be the main primitive? Why am I struggling with signature if digest is all I need?

You have a valid point about [ring](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/index.html), but [Signature](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/signature/index.html) and [Digest](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/digest/index.html#struct.Digest) serve different purposes. [Digest](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/digest/index.html#struct.Digest) handles hashing like SHA-256, while [Signature](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/signature/index.html) handles elliptic curve signing and verification using the [ring_ecdsa](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/ecdsa/index.html) feature. If you are only doing hashing, [Digest](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/digest/index.html#struct.Digest) is enough; if you need to sign messages, you must use [Signature](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/signature/index.html) and the corresponding key pair.
Okay so I should just import ring::signature::Signature and forget about pbkdf2? Can't I skip password hashing entirely?

No, absolutely not. [pbkdf2](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/pbkdf2/index.html) is essential for deriving keys from passwords securely. Without it, you risk exposing credentials via timing attacks or weak key derivation. You should use [ring::pbkdf2](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/pbkdf2/index.html#function.pbkdf2_hkdf) to derive a secret key from your password before passing it to [Signature](https://ring.rs/docs/ring/signature/index.html). This ensures compliance with modern crypto standards and protects against brute force attacks.

topic: Crypto - Ring Crate for Crypto Primitives

turns.content: I'm stuck on implementing a secure password hash in my new project and I tried using the standard library but it feels too slow compared to what we saw in class. Here is my current attempt: let digest = ring::digest::SHA256.as_mut_slice(); let key_material = some_string_bytes.into_iter().collect::<Vec<u8>>();

You're mixing up the crate names here. The standard library doesn't have a ring implementation, so you need to import from the ring crate directly using ring::digest and ring::pbkdf2 for password hashing instead of just SHA256 alone. The pbkdf2 module provides specific functions like ring::pbkdf2::HmacSha256 that are designed for deriving keys securely.

Oh I see, so ring::digest is just the hash output and ring::pbkdf2 handles the slow iteration? But why did my code crash with an error about missing trait implementations when I tried to call the digest function?

The error happened because you didn't use the pbkdf2 wrapper correctly. The ring::digest types are just raw data containers and don't implement the hashing logic by themselves; they need to be wrapped in a ring::pbkdf2 structure first before you can derive keys from them.
That makes sense now. So I should wrap my key material with ring::digest and then pass it into the pbkdf2 function? And how do I know which specific digest variant to use for compatibility?

Exactly, you wrap your input bytes in a ring::digest variant like ring::digest::HmacSha256 or ring::digest::SHA256 depending on your needs. For maximum compatibility and performance, stick with the Hmac variants provided by ring::pbkdf2 since they are designed to work seamlessly with the rest of the crypto primitives in that crate.

I tested it again using ring::digest::HmacSha256 and ring::pbkdf2::HmacSha256 and got a proper output without errors. It seems to be faster than what I was doing before. Is this the recommended approach for production systems?

Yes, using ring::digest combined with ring::pbkdf2 is the standard for secure password hashing in Rust because it follows the best practices from the crypto community and avoids common pitfalls like timing attacks or weak implementations.

topic: Crypto - Ring Crate for Crypto Primitives

turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I thought I just needed to import a library like serde_json or std::io, but ring::signature seems to require me to manually construct every byte and call impl Serialize on each primitive. It feels so fragile compared to the standard crate.

That frustration is common when transitioning from high-level crates; you are mistaken that you must manually serialize raw bytes for every operation because ring::digest actually provides a high-level API via the Digest trait that wraps these operations safely. Think of it like using a specialized wrench instead of trying to build a tool from individual screws and bolts; the crate handles the complex internal mechanics so you only interact with the surface level methods like hash_digest(). You do not need to call impl Serialize on each primitive manually because ring::signature is designed as a drop-in replacement for other crypto libraries where you simply instantiate the Signature trait. The real advantage here is that while some crates prioritize raw speed, ring prioritizes compliance by ensuring your code never accidentally leaks information through side channels.
So I can just swap in ring::pbkdf2 instead of scrypt and expect better performance? I thought the Ring library was slower because it avoided optimizations found in other crypto implementations.

Not at all; you cannot simply swap in ring::pbkdf2 for a direct speed increase because ring's design philosophy focuses on constant-time execution to prevent timing attacks rather than raw throughput. Imagine a highway where one lane is open for everyone versus a dedicated express lane with fewer cars; the Ring library avoids traffic jams (side-channel leaks) that slow down other implementations under pressure, ensuring your cryptographic operations remain secure even when scaled. This means you might find it slower in isolated benchmarks but safer and more reliable in production environments.
